author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.

lineage: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
Why Cain Has Been by Some Thought to Have Had Children by His Mother Eve. The Sins of Righteous Men. Who Can Be Both Righteous, and Yet Not Without Sin.
title: Why Cain Has Been by Some Thought to Have Had Children by His Mother Eve. The Sins of Righteous Men. Who Can Be Both Righteous, and Yet Not Without Sin.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Chapter 45 [XXXVIII.]—Why Cain Has Been by Some Thought to Have Had Children by His Mother Eve.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Who Can Be Both Righteous, and Yet Not Without Sin.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: When he says this, he forgets what he had himself said not long before: “After the human race had multiplied, it was possible that in the crowd the Scripture may have neglected to notice the sins of all men.”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: If indeed he had borne this well in mind, he would have seen that even in one man there was such a crowd and so vast a number of slight sins, that it would have been impossible (or, even if possible, not desirable) to describe them.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: For only such are recorded as the due bounds allowed, and as would, by few examples, serve for instructing the reader in the many cases where he needed warning.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Scripture has indeed omitted to mention concerning the few persons who were then in existence, either how many or who they were,—in other words, how many sons and daughters Adam and Eve begat, and what names they gave them; and from this circumstance some, not considering how many things are quietly passed over in Scripture, have gone so far as to suppose that Cain cohabited with his mother, and by her had the children which are mentioned, thinking that Adam’s sons had no sisters, because Scripture failed to mention them in the particular place, although it afterwards, in the way of recapitulation, implied what it had previously omitted,—that “Adam begat sons and daughters,” without, however, dropping a syllable to intimate either their number or the time when they were born.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: In like manner it was unnecessary to state whether Abel, notwithstanding that he is rightly styled “righteous,” ever indulged in immoderate laughter, or was ever jocose in moments of relaxation, or ever looked at an object with a covetous eye, or ever plucked fruit to extravagance, or ever suffered indigestion from too much eating, or ever in the midst of his prayers permitted his thoughts to wander and call him away from the purpose of his devotion; as well as how frequently these and many other similar failings stealthily crept over his mind.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: And are not these failings sins, about which the apostle’s precept gives us a general admonition that we should avoid and restrain them, when he says: “Let not sin therefore reign in your mortal body, that ye should obey it in the lusts thereof?”

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: To escape from such an obedience, we have to struggle in a constant and daily conflict against unlawful and unseemly inclinations.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Only let the eye be directed, or rather abandoned, to an object which it ought to avoid, and let the mischief strengthen and get the mastery, and adultery is consummated in the body, which is committed in the heart only so much more quickly as thought is more rapid than action and there is no impediment to retard and delay it.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: They who in a great degree have curbed this sin, that is, this appetite of a corrupt affection, so as not to obey its desires, nor to “yield their members to it as instruments of unrighteousness,” have fairly deserved to be called righteous persons, and this by the help of the grace of God.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: Since, however, sin often stole over them in very small matters, and when they were off their guard, they were both righteous, and at the same time not sinless.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: To conclude, if there was in righteous Abel that love of God whereby alone he is truly righteous who is righteous, to enable him, and to lay him under a moral obligation, to advance in holiness, still in whatever degree he fell short therein was of sin.

verses.book: A Treatise on Nature and Grace.
verses.text: And who indeed can help thus falling short, until he come to that mighty power thereof, in which man’s entire infirmity shall be swallowed up?
